note.soap:
S:
34-year-old adult presents with a tick bite sustained in Georgia. The tick was attached for approximately 37 hours. The patient reports an expanding bullseye-like rash consistent with erythema migrans. No systemic symptoms such as fever, chills, severe headache, body aches, or stiff neck are reported.

O:
No physical exam or vital signs available due to phone-only encounter.

A:
  - Working diagnosis (most likely): Early Lyme disease with erythema migrans rash.
  - Differential: Other tick-borne illnesses less likely given absence of systemic symptoms; cellulitis or allergic reaction less likely given characteristic rash.
- Red flags considered: No systemic symptoms reported (fever, chills, severe headache, body aches, stiff neck).

P:
  - Disposition: Outpatient management appropriate.
  - Plan: Recommended regimen; reviewing physician will confirm appropriateness and prescribe if indicated: doxycycline 100 mg PO twice daily for 10 days. Alternatives if doxycycline contraindicated or not tolerated include amoxicillin 500 mg PO three times daily for 14 days or cefuroxime axetil 500 mg PO twice daily for 14 days.
  - Safety net: Advise patient to seek urgent care if fever, worsening rash, new neurological symptoms, palpitations, chest pain, or difficulty breathing develop.
patient_instructions: If you are not improving as expected, or if symptoms worsen, seek in-person medical care.
Medication options you may consider if safe (no dosing by phone): acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you can take it safely; ibuprofen (Advil) if you can take it safely.
